text: In the elective rectal prolapse surgery, the patient is positioned in jackknife. A Lone Star retractor is placed to expose the surgical field, and Babcock forceps are used to avert the rectal prolapse. The dentin line is identified, and the prolapse is measured. An incision is made two centimeters above the dental line.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the rectum and mesorectum through a vascular plane, coagulating and interrupting small mesorectal vessels near the rectal wall.

In the emergency surgery, the surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the dentin line due to venous congestion and tissue edema. Bleeding occurs during the rectal section, prompting the use of single stitches and an ultrasound device for tissue manipulation instead of an electroscalpel.

The surgeon reaches the sigmoid colon and completes the circumferential section of the mesorectum. In the emergency scenario, the sigmoid colon is lifted using a plastic loop to facilitate the maneuver.

During the elective surgery, the surgeon performs a levatorplasty, addressing the diastasis of the levator ani muscle branches. A linear stapler is used to divide the sigmoid colon, visibly speeding up the procedure.

The surgeon performs a stitch-by-stitch coloanal anastomosis, meticulously connecting the colon to the anal canal. Following the completion of the anastomosis, the Lone Star retractor is removed from the surgical field.
In the emergency case, the surgeon performs a laparoscopic diverting colostomy, carefully navigating the altered tissue involved in the anastomosis to mitigate the high risk of leakage.
